coredata.dc:title: Impurity removal from Fe, Cr, Ti, and V metals by hydrogen plasma arc melting and thermodynamic estimation of hydride and sulfide formation
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Elanski, D.
Lim, J.-W.
Mimura, K.
Isshiki, M.
coredata.dc:description: Experimental data of Fe, Cr, Ti, and V metals purified by Ar and Ar-30vol% H2 plasma arc melting was compared with results of thermodynamic estimations. The Fe metal refined by Ar and Ar-30vol% H2 plasma gas at 101.3 and 5.3kPa had approximate 45% removal degree of the total impurities. On the other hand, Co, Ni, Zr, Nb, and Mo were not reduced from the Fe metal due to their lower vapor pressures than that of Fe. At 5.3kPa, hydrogen plasma arc melting of Ti and V, which have lower initial impurity contents than that of Fe, resulted in a removal degree of near 40% of impurities, while the average removal degree of impurities in the Cr metal close to 30%. Thermodynamic estimation could explain qualitatively a tendency of each impurity behavior. It was shown by the thermodynamic calculations that several impurities, namely Mn, S and P, form hydrides with relatively high pressures compared with pressures of atomic species. Furthermore, It was also confirmed that the formation of some sulfides in the gas phase including FeS, SiS and GeS affects the behavior of S and sulfide forming elements.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Plasma arc
Hydrogen
Iron
Chromium
Titanium
Vanadium
Refining
Thermodynamic estimation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Melting of Ti and V metals

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Calculated temperature dependence of the normalized impurity concentration in the Fe metal and experimental data at 101.3kPa.
Temperature dependence of the equilibrium partial pressure of selected gas species in the system of the Fe metal at 101.3kPa.
Temperature dependence of the equilibrium partial pressure of selected gas species in the system of the Fr metal at 5.3kPa.
Calculated temperature dependence of the normalized impurity concentration in the Cr metal and experimental data at 5.3kPa.
Temperature dependence of the equilibrium partial pressure of selected gas species in the system of the Cr metal at 5.3kPa.
Calculated temperature dependence of the normalized impurity concentration in the Ti metal and experimental data at 5.3kPa.
Temperature dependence of the equilibrium partial pressure of selected gas species in the system of the Ti metal at 5.3kPa.
Comparison of the experimental and equilibrium values of impurity removal degree for the V metal under 5.3kPa at 2950K.

Impurity contents before and after melting of the Fe metal and the average removal degrees of the impurities

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The marks “#” and “–” represent the case that content change is within the analysis accuracy, and the case that value was less than detection limit, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Impurity contents in Cr metal before and after melting by HPAM under the pressure of 5.3kPa, and the average removal degrees of the impurities
Impurity contents in Ti and V metals before and after melting by HPAM under the pressure of 5.3kPa, and the average removal degrees of the impurities

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The mark meaning is the same as in Table 2.

article.ce:title: Impurity removal from Fe, Cr, Ti, and V metals by hydrogen plasma arc melting and thermodynamic estimation of hydride and sulfide formation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Steel Electrometallurgy and Ferroalloys, Moscow State University of Steel and Alloys, Moscow 119049, Russia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Multidisciplinary Research for Advanced Materials, Tohoku University, Sendai 980-8577, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Experimental data of Fe, Cr, Ti, and V metals purified by Ar and Ar-30vol% H2 plasma arc melting was compared with results of thermodynamic estimations. The Fe metal refined by Ar and Ar-30vol% H2 plasma gas at 101.3 and 5.3kPa had approximate 45% removal degree of the total impurities. On the other hand, Co, Ni, Zr, Nb, and Mo were not reduced from the Fe metal due to their lower vapor pressures than that of Fe. At 5.3kPa, hydrogen plasma arc melting of Ti and V, which have lower initial impurity contents than that of Fe, resulted in a removal degree of near 40% of impurities, while the average removal degree of impurities in the Cr metal close to 30%. Thermodynamic estimation could explain qualitatively a tendency of each impurity behavior. It was shown by the thermodynamic calculations that several impurities, namely Mn, S and P, form hydrides with relatively high pressures compared with pressures of atomic species. Furthermore, It was also confirmed that the formation of some sulfides in the gas phase including FeS, SiS and GeS affects the behavior of S and sulfide forming elements.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, thermal plasma is extensively used for materials processing in industrial and laboratory scales, allowing a flexible choice of treatment conditions. The refining effect of hydrogen plasma arc melting (HPAM) has been found for Fe and steels [1–3], and for some refractory metals [1,4–6]. On the other hand, the removal of impurities from Fe and V by HPAM was confirmed only for a few impurities of mostly non-metals. Besides, since reported results have been achieved under various melting conditions so far, it is very difficult to compare properly behaviors of impurities among various metals or their degrees of purification. Furthermore, there is few data concerning purification effects of such Ti and Cr metals by HPAM.

In our previous work [7], it has been reported that the hydrogen addition to the plasma gas resulted in a better purification of Zr and Nb under both atmospheric and reduced pressures, which showed the removal degrees of the total amount of impurities from 50–55% to 60–80%. Particularly, 80% of the total amount of impurities from Ta metal was eliminated by HPAM at 101.3kPa. The results of thermodynamic calculations for Zr, Nb and Ta metals were in a qualitative agreement with the experimental data showing almost the same order of the impurities according to their removal degrees. The thermodynamic calculations showed that none of impurities forms hydrides prevailing over corresponding atoms in the gas phase above the examined metals. Therefore, it was confirmed that a main effect of H2 addition on the impurity removal is caused by the increase of the surface temperature of molten specimens. In this paper, experimental data of Fe, Cr, Ti and V purified by HPAM and results of thermodynamic estimations from equilibrium modelling of their systems will be described to show the removal effect of impurities and the influence of H2 addition on the composition of the metals and gas phases to clarify the purification mechanism of HPAM.

The plasma arc furnace equipped with a transferred arc type plasma torch was described in the previous report [7], A thoriated W cathode was placed in the plasma torch and a specimen mounted on the water-cooled copper crucible was utilized as an anode. High purity argon (>99.9995%) and H2 (>99.9999%) were used and experiments were carried out under the reduced (5.3kPa) and atmospheric pressures (101.3kPa). Specimens with a button shape were prepared by melting pure Fe, Cr, Ti and V under the Ar and Ar-H2 atmospheres. Table 1 shows the main experimental parameters of the plasma melting. The specimens were turned upside down for homogenization after plasma melting for 30min and thus total melting time was 60min. The gas flow and arc length were kept as 5L/min and 15mm, respectively. As the preliminary experiments, the arc power values required for melting source of refractory metals and their alloys were determined and the relation between the arc powers and their melting points was used as a reference curve to estimate the temperature of the specimens.

GDMS (VG ELEMENTAL: VG9000) was used for precise analysis of impurity contents in the specimens before and after plasma arc melting. For the description of experimental data, the removal degree (RD)% is defined for each impurity by Eq. (1).
(1)
RD =
100 (
X 0
−
X f
)
X 0
where X 0 and X f are the initial and final concentrations, respectively. In order to clarify the purification effect of the plasma melting, the average value of the removal degree (RDav)% is counted using Eq. (2).
(2)
R
D
av
=
1 m
∑ 1 m
RD
,
where m is the number of impurities which have been detected by GDMS.

For thermodynamic calculations, the multipurpose computer program ASTRA.4/pc with equilibrium determination based on the maximization of entropy of the system [8] was used. The assumptions for the calculation have been described in the previous report [7]. To simplify both a calculation procedure and analysis of results, only impurities possessing the initial concentration above 1 mass ppm were taken into account. For the result presentation, normalized concentration C is defined as a ratio of the impurity atomic fraction at the given temperature, X, to initial one, X 0:
(3)
C =
X
X 0
.

The value of RD is coupled with C at the appropriate temperature as:
(4)
RD = 100 ( 1 − C ) .

Analytical results of Fe specimens before and after melting under different conditions are presented in Table 2 . Compared with the previous Zr, Nb and Ta metals [7], the Fe specimen contained much more S and a certain amount of Ge. The impurities except Co, Ni, Zr, Nb and Mo, which are not listed in Table 2, could be removed from the Fe metal using both Ar plasma arc melting and HPAM. The RD value of most impurities was approximately between 20 and 50%. On the other hand, removal degrees more than 70% in the case of Na, Al, Si, S, Mn, Cd and Sb could be obtained in a certain melting condition. On the other hand, since the elements with refractory property such as Co, Ni, Zr, Nb and Mo have lower vapor pressures than that of Fe, it is very difficult to remove these elements from Fe metal by plasma arc melting, which resulted in the remaining impurities in the Fe metal.

The addition of H2 resulted in a better removal of P, S, Mn, As, Ag, Sn and Sb at both pressures of 5.3 and 101.3kPa, while a little change in the average removal degree was observed due to the lower removal degrees of Si, Ge and Cd impurities compared to the case of Ar plasma arc melting. As a result, there was no significant variation in the melting results for both pressures and types of the plasma gas. The RDav values were approximately 45%, which are remarkably lower than 50–80% obtained on the Zr, Nb and Ta metals. This result is attributed to the different vapor pressures of the melted metals. On the other hand, the desulfurization degrees achieved by HPAM in the present work were 80 and 90%, respectively. This result suggests a great possibility of desulfurization by the H2 addition to the plasma gas. Mimura et al. have reported that the higher removal degree of Sn from a Fe metal by HPAM, compared with the melting under Ar plasma arc melting, could be obtained [4]. Although the Fe metal had 3 orders of magnitude higher initial concentration than that in the Fe metal examined in the present work, the high RD values of Sn from both Fe metals with different initial concentrations could be obtained by HPAM, which suggests that H2 addition to Ar plasma gas has a great effect on the process of the impurity removal regardless of an initial concentration.

The calculated temperature dependence of the normalized impurity concentration in the Fe metal and the experimental data at 101.3kPa is shown in Fig. 1 . The term “normalized concentration” means that all impurity concentrations were started from 1.0 as a starting point and the temperature range discussed with experimental data in the present work was selected. In the previous report [7], the difference of the calculated impurity concentrations in the Zr or Nb metal between Ar and Ar-H2 plasma gases was negligible small and then, only one curve was used. However, the calculated concentrations of some impurities corresponding to Ar and Ar-H2 plasma gases were shown to have a difference in the case of Fe metal because the temperature range calculated in the present work was much lower than that for Zr and Nb metals. According to their behaviors shown in Fig. 1, the impurities removed from the Fe metal could be classified as follows.
(A)
Removed elements such as P, S, Mn, Cr, Cd and Al; the concentration of P, S, Mn depend on the plasma gas, while Cr, Cd and Al do not depend on the plasma gas composition.
(B)
Unremoved elements such as Ni, Co, Zr, Mo, Si and Ge; the concentration of Ni, Co, Zr and Mo does not depend on the plasma gas and increase even at high temperature. Furthermore, there is no concentrations change for Si and Ge at Ar-30% H2 plasma gas.

For P, Cr and Cd elements, experimental results were a good agreement with the thermodynamic estimation. The experimental data of Si, S and Ge were remarkably lower than the equilibrium values, while that of only Mn was higher than the calculated result. The deviation from the thermodynamic estimation will be explained by hydrides and sulfides formations below.

The formation of several hydrides and compounds causes the difference of metal compositions in equilibrium with Ar and Ar-H2 plasma gases. To clarify the effect of hydrides and other compounds formation, the partial pressures of the selected gas species in the equilibrium with the liquid Fe metal at 101.3 and 5.3kPa were examined and shown in Figs. 2 and 3
, respectively. According to the calculation, the partial pressures of Al, Cr, Mn, Ni, Cu, Zr, Mo and Cd atomic species were 2–8 orders of magnitude higher than those of the corresponding sulfides (not shown here). On the other hand, the sulfide formations of Si, Fe and Ge make a significant contribution to the sulfur distribution between liquid and gas phases and subsequently affect the behavior of Si and Ge. Ge at low temperature and Si were present in the gas phases mainly as GeS and SiS with the partial pressures higher than those of their atoms. Their partial pressures do not rise in accordance with the pressure increase of atomic S, Ge and Si, since GeS and SiS trend to dissociate at higher temperatures. The transfer of Si and Ge to the gas phase as sulfides providing the effective removal of gaseous sulfides from the metal surface should be a main reason for the significant difference between experimental and calculated results. However, by adding H2 to Ar plasma gas may cause a difficulty to remove Si and Ge as sulfides due to the decrease of the S partial pressure by SH and H2S formations, particularly at 101.3kPa.

As shown in Fig. 2, the hydride formation can promote the transfer of S, P and Mn to the gas phase under Ar+30% H2 condition at 101.3kPa because MnH, SH and H2S prevail over the corresponding atoms and PH has a similar partial pressure to that of the atomic P in the examined temperature range. This indicates that Ar-H2 plasma allows to remove these impurities superior to pure Ar plasma and such expectation coincides with the experimental results shown in Fig. 1. In the case of Al, Si, Cr, Co, Ni and Zr elements, their behaviors is not affected so much by the H2 addition due to relatively low partial pressures of their hydrides. On the other hand, the molecular particles in the gas phase at 5.3kPa tend to dissociate (see Fig. 3), so that the partial pressures of hydrides are lower than those in the system at 101.3kPa. In this condition, since S and Mn prevail over SH, H2S and MnH, a main driving force of the impurity removal comes from the difference in vapor pressures between the element and the metal. Compared with the results on Zr, Nb and Ta metals [7], the formation of several gaseous compounds such as sulfides and hydrides is important for Fe metal to explain the impurity removal between the gas phases and the metal in the system.

The impurity concentrations in the Cr specimen before and after melting by HPAM are given in Table 3 . Among the examined metals including Zr, Nb and Ta metals described in the previous paper [7], the Cr metal contains the highest amount of S, P, Mn, Ni, Fe, Ga and Zr (19ppm) impurities and has high initial levels of Si and Nb (194ppm).

Although the melting of Cr under the pressure of 5.3kPa by HPAM was accompanied with the strong vaporization of the specimen, the concentration of many impurities was not decreased. Fe, Co, Ni, Zr, Nb and Mo remained in the Cr metal after the melting by HPAM. On the contrary, it was found that the concentrations of Fe, Co, and Ni impurities considerably increased by a high Cr vaporization. On the other hand, the contents of As, In, Sb and Pb were reduced over 80%, and the concentrations of B, Na, Mg, S, Sc Mn, Cu, Ga and Sn were decreased down to 40–60%. Besides, the removal degrees of Si and P were smaller compared to the case of the Fe specimen. Since Cr has a boiling point closed to the melting point with the difference of about 770K, it is likely to boil the specimen on the surface at the melting condition used in the study while relatively large part of the specimen near the crucible is solid state. Therefore, the plasma melting of Cr is not effective for the purpose of purification due to the weight loss by high vaporization of the melted metal. The purification of Cr is the poorest among the studied metals; RDav of the Cr metal does not exceed 30% due to the increase in the concentrations of several impurities.

The normalized impurity concentrations in the Cr metal compared with the experimental data are shown in Fig. 4 , and the partial pressures of the selected gas species in the equilibrium with the liquid Cr metal are demonstrated in Fig. 5 . The experimental removal degrees of all the elements except Si and Sn were lower than those of the calculated results, and the enrichment of Ni, Fe, Zr, Nb and Mo due to the Cr vaporization was less than expected equilibrium values. These differences maybe caused by a difficulty in Cr plasma melting resulted from the small difference between the melting and the boiling points (high vapor pressure at melting point). In the gas phase, the partial pressure of SiS is higher than that of atomic Si (see Fig. 5), which is similar to the result obtained from the Fe metal. The formation of SiS and CrS significantly contributes to the sulfur distribution between Cr metal and gas phases because of the decrease of the S partial pressure as explained above, which suggests that the transfer of Si and Cr to the gas phase as sulfides results in an effective removal of Si and S from the metal surface. On the other hand, none of other impurities forms hydride or sulfide with relatively high partial pressure. As is similar to the result on the Fe metal at 5.3kPa, since the pressures of SH and H2S are lower than that of S in the Cr metal, the addition of H2 to the plasma gas does not cause a better removal of S impurity from the Cr metal due to the low partial pressures of S hydrides.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Melting of Ti and V metals
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical results of Ti and V metals before and after the melting by HPAM under the pressure of 5.3kPa are shown in Table 4 . In the case of the Ti metal, besides the listed impurities, the elements of Y, Zr, Nb and Mo remained in the Ti metal after the melting. The removal degree of Mg, P, Mn and Pb was above 70% and the RD values of S, Cr, Co, Ni, Ga, Cd and In were 40–70%. The lower values of RD were obtained on Al, Fe, Sn and Sb. The impurities such as V, Zr, Nb and Mo have the lower vapor pressures than that of Ti so that they could not be eliminated from the Ti metal.

On the other hand, the V metal has the highest Al and Si concentrations among the examined base metals. The RD values of S, Mn and Cu was above 70% and Al, P, Cr, Fe, Ga, In Sb and Pb had the RD values of 40–70%. The smaller values of RD were obtained on Si, Co, Ni, Cd and Sn. Several elements such as Ti, Y, Zr, Nb and Mo were not removed as the same result of the Ti metal. The value of RDav for the impurities in the Ti and V metals were close to about 38%, which was larger than that of the Cr metal. Because it was possible to remove Fe, Ni and Co from the Ti metal that could not reduced from the Cr metal. Our previous result [6] showed that when a crude V with high initial oxygen concentration was purified under the pressure of 6.6kPa using Ar-40vol% H2 plasma gas, the RD values of Al, Fe and Si impurities were 95, 30 and 35%, respectively. Compared to the present result, the RD values of those impurities were 68, 40 and 18%, respectively, which were lower than the values of the previous result. This difference is considered attributable to the high oxygen content in the crude V that contributes to a better removal of Al, Fe and Si due to the formation of volatile oxides such as Al2O and SiO.

The normalized impurity concentrations in the Ti metal compared with the experimental data and the partial pressures of the selected gas species are shown in Figs. 6 and 7
, respectively. The impurities concentrations obtain from the experimental data as a whole were sightly higher than those estimated by the thermodynamic calculation. The calculated result of the normalized impurity concentration in the V metal (not shown here) was also similar to that in the case of the Ti metal shown in Fig. 6. The most of gaseous compounds have lower partial pressures than those of the corresponding atoms. Only at the lower temperatures, the partial pressure of SiS is higher than that of the atomic Si and TiS sulfide with high partial pressure prevails over the others. The steep decreases of all the partial pressures above the high temperature of 2900K is considered to be due to the increase of the total number of gas particles caused by H2 dissociation and the high vaporization of the Ti metal. Fig. 8 shows the comparison of the experimental and the equilibrium RD values of the V metal estimated at the temperature of 2950K. The calculated results agree with the experimental data in the tendency of the impurity removal, whereas the experimental values of RD were lower than those predicted by the calculation. In the contrast with the Fe, Cr and Ti metals, none of the impurities in the V specimen forms a chemical compound with a partial pressure higher than that of the corresponding atoms.

From the fitting the estimated results to the experimental data above-described for Fe, Cr, Ti and V metals, it is established that the thermodynamic estimation can suggest a tendency on the removal and behavior of each impurity at a focused temperature range although there is a slight deviation between the experimental data and the thermodynamic calculation.
Hydrogen plasma arc melting was carried out on the commercial Fe, Cr, Ti and V metals. The removal degree of impurities was investigated and compared with results of thermodynamic calculations.

The Fe metal melted by Ar and Ar-30vol% H2 plasma gas at 101.3 and 5.3kPa had about 45% RDav of the total impurities. On the other hand, Co, Ni, Zr, Nb and Mo were not reduced from the Fe metal due to their lower vapor pressures than that of Fe. In the case of the Cr metal, it was found that HPAM of Cr metal is not effective due to the weight loss by high Cr vaporization, which resulted in the low RDav less than 30%. HPAM of the Ti and the V metals, which have lower initial impurity contents than the Fe specimen, resulted in the RDav of 38%. The results of thermodynamic calculations are generally in a good agreement with the experimental data concerning the tendency of the impurity removal from the metals. According to the calculation, only a few elements such as S, P and Mn in the Fe or the Cr specimens can form hydrides, which contributes to the impurity distribution between the metal and the gas phases. Furthermore, the formation of sulfides of Si, Ge in the gas phases affects the behavior of S as well as the sulfide forming impurities. The formation such as sulfides and hydrides is important to explain the impurity removal between the gas phases and the metal in the system.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phenomena of Plasma Arc alloying and Refining of Metals

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: K. Kaneko, N. Sano, Y. Matsushita, Tetsu-to-Hagane, J. Iron Steel Inst. Jpn. 63 (1977) 16.
T. Matsuo, Tetsu-to-Hagane, J. Iron Steel Inst. Jpn. 75 (1989) 82.

1st International Conference Process. Mater for Properties The Minerals Metals and Materials Society (TMS)

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: D. Elanski, J.-W. Lim, M. Mimura, M. Isshik, J. Alloys. Comp.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Using of Computers in Metallurgical Processes Calculations
